content.text: Every agentic AI business case rests on a stack of assumptions: how many tasks the agent will handle, how often it will succeed without human intervention, how much time each automated task saves, and how quickly adoption will ramp. When those assumptions prove optimistic, the realized value falls short of the projected value. Value realization analysis closes this gap by making every assumption explicit, quantifying its impact through sensitivity analysis, and tracking actual versus projected outcomes through a defined post-deployment measurement program.

attrs.alt: Tornado sensitivity diagram showing how ±20% changes in each assumption affect 3-year NPV, ranked from automation rate (highest impact) down to annual overhead (lowest impact)

content.text: What It Is

Value realization assumptions are the explicit, documented inputs that drive the projected benefits in an agentic AI business case — the automation rate, minutes saved per task, adoption timeline, FTE displacement fraction, and error reduction rate, among others. Sensitivity analysis is the technique for quantifying how much each assumption, when varied independently, moves the outcome metric (typically 3-year NPV or payback period). Together, these practices form a structured link between the projection in the business case and the measured outcomes after deployment.

A value realization framework typically has three components: an assumption register that documents every input with its source and confidence level; a sensitivity model (often visualized as a tornado diagram) that ranks assumptions by their impact on the outcome; and a post-deployment tracking dashboard that measures actuals against each assumption and triggers reviews when variances exceed defined thresholds.
Why It Matters

Most AI program benefit shortfalls trace to a small number of over-optimistic assumptions rather than to technical failure. Teams routinely underestimate the training time required before users adopt new agent-assisted workflows (increasing the ramp period), overestimate the fraction of tasks the agent can fully automate (confusing prototype performance with production performance across all task varieties), and underestimate the time staff continue to spend reviewing agent outputs even after the agent handles the primary work.

Sensitivity analysis prevents value leakage by identifying which assumptions most directly drive the outcome before the program launches. If the tornado diagram shows that automation rate has three times the NPV impact of any other variable, the program team knows to invest monitoring effort in that metric first, and to build adoption strategies that address the specific barriers to automation rate achievement — not to optimize for factors that barely move the needle.
Benefits
Early risk identification:
High-sensitivity assumptions become the primary risk factors in the program risk register, getting mitigation plans before deployment.
Focused monitoring:
Teams instrument and track the few metrics that matter most, rather than trying to measure everything equally.
Credible forecasts:
Documented assumption sources (measured vs. estimated vs. benchmarked) allow reviewers to challenge inputs rather than guess at the model logic.
Mid-course correction triggers:
Pre-defined variance thresholds automatically trigger reviews before small shortfalls compound into large ones.
Post-program learning:
Comparing projected to actual assumptions builds a calibration dataset that improves the accuracy of future AI business cases.
Trade-offs
Analysis paralysis risk:
Sensitivity analysis can expand to test every possible assumption combination, creating infinite model iterations without improving the decision quality.
Assumption dependency:

Single-factor sensitivity (varying one assumption while holding others fixed) can be misleading when assumptions are correlated — e.g., higher adoption rate typically co-occurs with higher task volume.
Measurement overhead:
Tracking actuals against all documented assumptions requires instrumentation investment and ongoing analyst time that must be budgeted as part of program overhead.
How It Works: Sensitivity Mechanics

One-way sensitivity analysis holds all assumptions at their baseline values and varies one at a time across a defined range (typically ±20% or between a low and high estimate). For each variation, it computes the change in the output metric, typically 3-year NPV. The results are plotted in a tornado diagram, ranked by absolute impact from largest to smallest.

For assumptions with non-linear sensitivity (e.g., automation rate above the break-even threshold has outsized impact), the analysis is extended to cover the full feasible range rather than just ±20%. The break-even value of each high-sensitivity assumption is also computed: the specific value at which NPV crosses zero. This defines the minimum required performance for each critical assumption.

attrs.latex: \text{Break-even automation rate} = \frac{\text{Annual TCO}}{V_{\text{annual}} \times r_{\text{handle time}} \times C_{\text{FTE/hr}}}

content.text: Where
is annual task volume,
is the handle time saving per task in hours, and
is the loaded FTE cost per hour. This is the minimum automation rate at which the program breaks even on an annual cash basis.
Architecture: Assumption Register and Tracking System

The value realization system has three layers. The assumption register is a structured table of every assumption with its value, source, confidence level, and sensitivity rank. The scenario model is a spreadsheet or code model that outputs NPV and payback period for any combination of input values, enabling both sensitivity analysis and scenario comparison. The tracking dashboard is a live system (often a BI tool connected to the agent's instrumentation) that plots actuals against projected values monthly, with alert thresholds that trigger review workflows when variance exceeds 15%.
When to Use It

Value realization analysis is most valuable at two points. First, during the business case development phase, it reveals which assumptions need the most rigorous measurement before the investment decision, and may prompt the team to conduct targeted experiments (A/B tests with the agent on a subset of tasks) to tighten the range of the highest-sensitivity inputs. Second, during the post-deployment tracking phase, it serves as the measurement framework that operationalizes the commitment made to sponsors in the business case.
How to Implement
Step 1: Build the Assumption Register

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

ConfidenceLevel = Literal["measured", "estimated", "benchmarked", "assumed"]

@dataclass
class Assumption:
    name: str
    baseline_value: float
    unit: str
    low_value: float           # conservative estimate (pessimistic)
    high_value: float          # optimistic estimate
    confidence: ConfidenceLevel
    source: str                # how the value was obtained
    sensitivity_rank: int = 0  # filled in after sensitivity analysis

    def variation_range(self) -> tuple[float, float]:
        """Return (low, high) as fractions of baseline."""
        return (self.low_value / self.baseline_value, self.high_value / self.baseline_value)

# Typical assumptions for an agentic customer-service workflow
ASSUMPTIONS = [
    Assumption(
        name="automation_rate",
        baseline_value=0.65,
        unit="fraction",
        low_value=0.40,
        high_value=0.82,
        confidence="estimated",
        source="Prototype achieved 0.78 on 200-task sample; production discounted by 17% for edge cases",
    ),
    Assumption(
        name="minutes_saved_per_task",
        baseline_value=8.0,
        unit="minutes",
        low_value=5.0,
        high_value=12.0,
        confidence="measured",
        source="Time-motion study across 50 live tasks; analyst vs. agent-assisted handle times",
    ),
    Assumption(
        name="fte_loaded_cost",
        baseline_value=60.0,
        unit="USD/hour",
        low_value=52.0,
        high_value=70.0,
        confidence="benchmarked",
        source="HR compensation data; includes salary, benefits, office overhead",
    ),
    Assumption(
        name="daily_task_volume",
        baseline_value=400.0,
        unit="tasks/day",
        low_value=280.0,
        high_value=520.0,
        confidence="measured",
        source="Current ticket system; Q4 average with ±30% seasonal range",
    ),
    Assumption(
        name="build_cost",
        baseline_value=180_000.0,
        unit="USD one-time",
        low_value=180_000.0,  # sunk cost; fixed
        high_value=180_000.0,
        confidence="measured",
        source="Actual project estimate from engineering team",
    ),
]
Step 2: Run One-Way Sensitivity Analysis

from typing import Callable

def compute_npv(
    automation_rate: float,
    minutes_saved: float,
    fte_cost_hr: float,
    daily_tasks: float,
    build_cost: float,
    annual_overhead: float = 80_000.0,
    monthly_token_cost: float = 12_000.0,
    years: int = 3,
) -> float:
    """Simplified 3-year NPV (no discount rate for clarity)."""
    annual_tasks = daily_tasks * 250  # working days
    annual_benefit = annual_tasks * automation_rate * (minutes_saved / 60) * fte_cost_hr
    annual_cost = monthly_token_cost * 12 + annual_overhead
    net_annual = annual_benefit - annual_cost
    return net_annual * years - build_cost


def one_way_sensitivity(
    baseline_params: dict,
    param_ranges: dict[str, tuple[float, float]],  # param -> (low, high)
    outcome_fn: Callable[..., float],
) -> list[dict]:
    """Vary each parameter independently and compute NPV delta."""
    baseline_npv = outcome_fn(**baseline_params)
    results = []

    for param, (low, high) in param_ranges.items():
        low_params = {**baseline_params, param: low}
        high_params = {**baseline_params, param: high}
        low_npv = outcome_fn(**low_params)
        high_npv = outcome_fn(**high_params)
        results.append({
            "parameter": param,
            "baseline_npv": round(baseline_npv),
            "low_npv": round(low_npv),
            "high_npv": round(high_npv),
            "low_delta": round(low_npv - baseline_npv),
            "high_delta": round(high_npv - baseline_npv),
            "total_range": round(high_npv - low_npv),
        })

    # Sort by total range descending (tornado ordering)
    return sorted(results, key=lambda x: abs(x["total_range"]), reverse=True)


baseline = {
    "automation_rate": 0.65,
    "minutes_saved": 8.0,
    "fte_cost_hr": 60.0,
    "daily_tasks": 400.0,
    "build_cost": 180_000.0,
}
param_ranges = {
    "automation_rate": (0.40, 0.82),
    "minutes_saved": (5.0, 12.0),
    "fte_cost_hr": (52.0, 70.0),
    "daily_tasks": (280.0, 520.0),
    "build_cost": (150_000.0, 240_000.0),
}

results = one_way_sensitivity(baseline, param_ranges, compute_npv)
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['parameter']:25s}: [{r['low_delta']:+,} to {r['high_delta']:+,}]  range={r['total_range']:,}")
Step 3: Track Actuals vs. Assumptions Post-Deployment

from datetime import date
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class AssumptionTracker:
    assumption_name: str
    projected_value: float
    alert_threshold_pct: float = 0.15  # trigger review at 15% variance
    actuals: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def record_actual(
        self, period: date, actual_value: float, notes: str = ""
    ) -> None:
        variance_pct = (actual_value - self.projected_value) / self.projected_value
        alert = abs(variance_pct) > self.alert_threshold_pct
        self.actuals.append({
            "period": period.isoformat(),
            "actual": actual_value,
            "projected": self.projected_value,
            "variance_pct": round(variance_pct * 100, 1),
            "alert": alert,
            "notes": notes,
        })
        if alert:
            print(
                f"ALERT [{self.assumption_name}] {period}: "
                f"actual={actual_value:.2f} vs projected={self.projected_value:.2f} "
                f"({variance_pct:+.0%}) — review required"
            )

    def latest_variance(self) -> Optional[float]:
        return self.actuals[-1]["variance_pct"] if self.actuals else None


# Set up trackers for high-sensitivity assumptions
automation_tracker = AssumptionTracker("automation_rate", projected_value=0.65)
handle_time_tracker = AssumptionTracker("minutes_saved_per_task", projected_value=8.0)

# Month 1 actuals
automation_tracker.record_actual(date(2025, 2, 28), actual_value=0.51,
    notes="Lower than expected; manual override rate high for billing queries")
handle_time_tracker.record_actual(date(2025, 2, 28), actual_value=7.2,
    notes="Slightly below estimate; agent drafts reviewed before sending")

# Month 2
automation_tracker.record_actual(date(2025, 3, 31), actual_value=0.58,
    notes="Improving; training batch 2 deployed")
Examples
Example 1: Break-Even Automation Rate Computation

def break_even_automation_rate(
    annual_tco: float,
    annual_tasks: float,
    minutes_saved_per_task: float,
    fte_cost_per_hour: float,
) -> float:
    """Minimum automation rate for annual cash break-even."""
    hourly_savings_per_task = (minutes_saved_per_task / 60) * fte_cost_per_hour
    return annual_tco / (annual_tasks * hourly_savings_per_task)

rate = break_even_automation_rate(
    annual_tco=224_000,       # token + overhead
    annual_tasks=400 * 250,
    minutes_saved_per_task=8,
    fte_cost_per_hour=60,
)
print(f"Break-even automation rate: {rate:.1%}")  # 0.28 = 28%
# Any automation rate above 28% generates positive annual cash flow
Real-World Example

An insurance claims processing team ran sensitivity analysis before deploying an agentic triage system. The tornado diagram revealed that automation rate was the dominant variable (±35% NPV swing), followed by average time-per-claim saved (±22%), and then model pricing (±9%). The team redesigned the escalation thresholds to maximize automation rate for straightforward claims, while accepting that complex multi-party claims would remain fully manual — explicitly locking in a conservative automation rate assumption for those claim types rather than averaging them into the overall rate.

Post-deployment, the team tracked automation rate weekly. When month-2 actuals showed 42% automation against a 58% projection, the alert triggered a root-cause review. The analysis revealed that a 14% subset of claims involved a third-party portal the agent could not access. Fixing the integration raised the automation rate to 61% by month 4 — still below the 65% baseline but well above the 40% conservative floor. The projected NPV was revised downward by 12%, which sponsors had anticipated as a possible outcome given the documented conservative scenario.
Common Mistakes
Treating prototype automation rate as production baseline:
Prototypes use curated tasks; production includes tail-end edge cases. Discount prototype performance by 15–25% for the baseline assumption.
Ignoring correlated assumptions:
Automation rate and task volume growth are often correlated — higher automation rates attract more task routing to the agent. Single-factor sensitivity understates the combined impact.
Setting thresholds too wide:
A 30% variance threshold triggers too few reviews; a 5% threshold generates alert fatigue. 10–15% is a practical starting point for quarterly reviews.
Tracking outputs rather than assumptions:
Measuring revenue impact rather than automation rate makes root-cause diagnosis impossible. Track the assumption inputs that drive the output, not just the output.
Best Practices
Run sensitivity analysis before finalizing the business case, not after — the results should influence which assumptions get additional measurement effort during discovery.

Label each assumption with its confidence level: measured (from data), estimated (from judgment), benchmarked (from industry data), or assumed (placeholder pending data). Prioritize converting assumed and estimated inputs to measured before the investment decision.
Compute and document the break-even value for each top-3 sensitivity assumption. This is the number the program must not fall below for the investment to remain viable.
Revisit the sensitivity rankings quarterly as the program scales. The set of high-sensitivity assumptions often changes as the program matures and early uncertainties resolve.
Archive actual vs. projected data from completed programs to build an organization-specific calibration database for future AI business cases.
Performance and Scalability

As an agent program scales to multiple workflows and departments, the assumption register grows from a dozen entries to hundreds. Managing sensitivity analysis at this scale requires parameterizing the model programmatically (as shown above) rather than maintaining separate spreadsheet scenarios, and grouping assumptions by workflow so that each workstream team owns the monitoring of their own inputs. Federated tracking dashboards — one per workflow with roll-up to program level — keep monitoring manageable without centralizing all decision-making.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Common tools for the assumption register and sensitivity model include Excel/Google Sheets for smaller programs, and Python (pandas, scipy) for programmatic sensitivity sweeps that feed BI dashboards. For post-deployment tracking, purpose-built AI program management platforms (such as AIMGA or custom dashboards in Tableau/Power BI connected to agent telemetry systems) provide automated variance alerting. LLM observability platforms (LangSmith, Helicone) provide the raw automation rate and token data that feeds the tracking layer.
Comparison with Alternatives
Method
Granularity
Correlated inputs
Best for
One-way sensitivity (tornado)
Per-variable ranking
Ignored
Identifying which assumptions need attention

2 variables at a time

Security and Reliability

The assumption register and sensitivity model contain projected financial data and business performance metrics that may be commercially sensitive. Store them in access-controlled environments with audit logging. When sharing with vendors or consultants, redact absolute dollar amounts and express assumptions as ratios where possible. For programs subject to financial reporting or regulatory scrutiny, the assumption register and tracking records may constitute evidence of management oversight of AI risk — ensure they are version-controlled and retained per the organization's records management policy.
Mental Model

Think of the assumption register as a contract between the program team and the investment sponsors. Every number in the business case traces back to an assumption in the register. When actuals diverge from projections, the first question is always 'which assumption proved incorrect?' — not 'why did the NPV miss?' The tornado diagram reveals which assumptions to watch most closely, in the same way that a structural engineer knows which load-bearing elements are most critical to inspect. Tracking actuals against assumptions converts the post-deployment phase from a narrative ('adoption is growing') to an evidence-based management discipline.
Key Takeaways
Every projected benefit in an AI business case depends on explicit assumptions; documenting them with sources makes the case auditable and makes variance analysis possible.
The tornado diagram reveals which 2–3 assumptions drive 80% of the NPV variability — concentrate measurement and monitoring effort on those.
Compute the break-even value of each high-sensitivity assumption before funding; this defines the minimum acceptable performance level for the program to remain viable.
Post-deployment, track assumption inputs (automation rate, handle time) rather than just output metrics (revenue impact) to enable fast root-cause diagnosis when variances appear.
Prototype automation rates should be discounted by 15–25% for the baseline assumption, because prototypes use curated tasks that underrepresent production edge cases.
Archive assumption actuals across programs to build an organizational calibration dataset that improves the accuracy of future AI investment cases.
